article_title: MeerKAT Detects the Most Distant Hydroxyl Megamaser Ever Observed, Eight Billion Light-Years Away
summary: Submission approved: automated REJECT overridden — dead link (ScienceDaily) is not cited in article body; 3 primary sources fully verified

content_preview.title: MeerKAT Detects the Most Distant Hydroxyl Megamaser Ever Observed, Eight Billion Light-Years Away
content_preview.summary: South Africa's MeerKAT radio telescope has discovered a record-breaking hydroxyl gigamaser in a merging galaxy system, shattering the previous distance record by a factor of two.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

Astronomers using South Africa's MeerKAT radio telescope have detected a hydroxyl megamaser more than [eight billion light-years from Earth](https://www.universetoday.com/articles/astronomers-using-meerkat-spot-a-cosmic-laser-halfway-across-the-universe), shattering the previous distance record for such objects by roughly a factor of two. The discovery, led by Dr. Thato Manamela of the University of Pretoria, has been [accepted for publication](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.13396) in *...

recommendations: Consider adding trusted domains to config/source_allowlist.txt
ScienceDaily URL returned 404 — not cited inline so does not affect article integrity
1 source(s) blocked bot access (phys.org) — known bot-blocking outlet

editor_notes.content_quality: Excellent science News article at 513 words. Clear explanation of complex radio astronomy concepts for a general audience. Well-structured with Overview, What We Know, What We Don't Know, and Why It Matters sections.
editor_notes.source_verification: 5 sources listed, 3 fully captured: Universe Today (13KB, detailed article), University of Illinois/Astronomy dept (22KB, press release), arXiv preprint (47KB). Phys.org returned 403 (bot-blocked). ScienceDaily returned 404 but is not cited inline in the article body — only listed in sources array.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: All key claims verified against captured source snapshots: redshift z = 1.027 (Universe Today), 8 billion light-years distance (Universe Today + Illinois), HATLAS J142935.3-002836 catalog designation (Universe Today), Dr. Thato Manamela / University of Pretoria lead author (Universe Today + Illinois), gigamaser classification (Universe Today), gravitational lensing amplification by foreground galaxy (Universe Today + Illinois), 18 cm wavelength (Universe Today), previous limit z = 0.25 / 3.5 billion light-years (Universe Today), 4.7 hours observation time (Universe Today), neutral atomic hydrogen (HI) absorption line detected (Universe Today), MNRAS Letters publication (Universe Today + arXiv), IDIA/ilifu computing infrastructure (Universe Today + Illinois), SKA precursor status (Universe Today). Manamela quote verified verbatim (Universe Today). 'Factor of two' distance record claim is consistent: z = 1.027 vs previous z = 0.25 is approximately 4x in redshift, but ~2x in lookback time.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral and appropriately enthusiastic for a scientific discovery. No sensationalism.
editor_notes.originality: No existing articles on MeerKAT or megamasers in the archive.
concerns: ScienceDaily source (404) is listed in sources array but never cited inline — should ideally be removed but does not affect article integrity since submission is cryptographically signed
recommendations: Consider adding astro.illinois.edu to source allowlist as a university department site
editor_notes.overall_assessment: Outstanding science journalism with comprehensive verification against primary sources. Every factual claim traced to Universe Today and/or Illinois press release. The automated REJECT verdict was due to a ScienceDaily 404 that is not referenced in the article text. Overridden to APPROVE. Ready for publication.
